lessons.title: std::hazard_pointer — Safe reclamation in lock-free structures
lessons.motivation:
Lock-free data structures are attractive: they avoid mutexes and promise scalability under high load. But the absence of locks creates a new hazard — **use-after-free**. When one thread deletes a list node, another thread that just read a pointer to that node may dereference already-freed memory.

Using `std::shared_ptr` as a fix seems natural, but `atomic<shared_ptr<T>>` requires an atomic increment of the reference count on every read — that operation itself becomes a bottleneck under high contention.

**Hazard pointers** are a mechanism for safe memory reclamation in lock-free structures, standardized in C++26 (P0566/P2530). A thread that wants to dereference a shared pointer first *registers* it as "hazardous". The reclaiming side must check all registered hazard pointers before deleting an object. If the object's address appears in any slot, deletion is deferred.
lessons.theory:
**Note:** `std::hazard_pointer` (`<hazard_pointer>`, P0566/P2530) is included in C++26 but has not yet been implemented by any compiler. All code examples conform to the specification.

## The problem: use-after-free in lock-free code

A race condition when freeing a pointer in a lock-free stack:

```
Thread A: reads top → gets pointer P to node N
Thread B: pops N off the stack → calls delete N
Thread A: dereferences P → CRASH (use-after-free)
```

The critical window: between reading a pointer and using it, another thread deletes the object. A mutex solves this at the cost of blocking. `atomic<shared_ptr<T>>` solves this at the cost of an atomic refcount increment on every `load` — under heavy read load this operation becomes a bottleneck. Hazard pointers avoid both.

## The hazard pointer idea

Before dereferencing a shared pointer, a thread *registers* that pointer as "hazardous": "I am using this address, do not free the object." The reclaimer must check all registered hazard pointers before `delete`. If the object's address is found in any slot, deletion is deferred.

Algorithm (reader side):
1. Load pointer `P` from an atomic variable.
2. Register `P` in the hazard pointer slot.
3. Re-read the atomic variable — if `P` still matches (no ABA swap happened), it is now safe to dereference `P`.
4. Use the object via `P`.
5. Clear the hazard pointer slot.

Algorithm (reclaimer side):
- When `retire()` is called on an object, the runtime scans all hazard pointer slots in the domain. If the address is found — deletion is deferred. A retry happens later (or at domain destruction).

## C++26 API

### `std::hazard_pointer_domain`

Tracks all hazard pointers and pending retirements. `std::hazard_pointer_default_domain()` returns the process-wide default domain. Custom domains can be created for isolation.

### `std::hazard_pointer`

A guard object. One `hazard_pointer` protects one pointer at a time. Obtained via `std::make_hazard_pointer(domain)`.

### `hp.protect(atomic_ptr)`

Atomically loads from `atomic<T*>` and registers the result as hazardous. Returns the loaded `T*`. Must be used instead of a plain `.load()`. Pattern with re-check:

```cpp
T* p;
do {
    p = hp.protect(shared_atomic_ptr);
} while (p != shared_atomic_ptr.load(std::memory_order_acquire));
// now p is safe to dereference
```

Why the loop? Between `hp.protect()` and the check, another thread may have replaced the pointer in the atomic variable. If the value changed, the registration protects the wrong object — repeat.

### `hp.reset_protection()`

Clears the hazard pointer slot (object no longer protected). Call after you are done using the pointer.

### `std::hazard_pointer_obj_base<T, Deleter>`

CRTP base class for objects to be protected by hazard pointers. Adds the `retire()` method.

### `obj.retire()`

Schedules `obj` for deletion. The runtime defers the actual `delete` until no hazard pointer in the domain points to this object.

## Performance model

| Operation | Cost |
|---|---|
| `hp.protect()` + check | O(1) — one atomic store + load + compare |
| `retire()` | O(N) — scan all slots, N = threads × HP per thread |
| Memory | O(threads × K), K — HP per thread (usually 1–2) |

Compared to `atomic<shared_ptr<T>>`: no atomic refcount increment on every read. Under heavy read load, hazard pointers scale significantly better.

## When to use

- Lock-free singly and doubly linked lists
- Lock-free stacks and queues
- Any pointer-based lock-free structure where reclamation safety matters
- **Not suitable** for trees with complex reachability (use epoch-based reclamation instead)

examples.title: Safe reading with hp.protect()
examples.explanation: `hp.protect(global_node)` atomically loads the pointer and registers it as hazardous. The do-while loop ensures no ABA swap occurred between registration and the check. After `reset_protection()`, the reclaimer may safely delete the object.

examples.title: Writer retiring an old node via retire()
examples.explanation: `writer_update()` atomically replaces the node, then calls `retire()` on the old one. The runtime scans all hazard pointer slots in the domain. As long as any reader holds a hazard pointer to `old_node`, deletion is deferred. Once all readers call `reset_protection()`, the object is deleted.

options: To confirm the object was not deleted by another thread before the pointer was registered
To increment the reference count
It is a compiler requirement for inlining
To flush the cache line before access
exercises.explanation: Between `hp.protect()` (which registers the pointer) and the check, another thread may have atomically replaced the pointer in `global_ptr`. If the value changed, the hazard pointer slot protects the wrong object — the loop must restart with the new value. The re-check detects exactly this race.

exercises.prompt: What does `obj.retire()` do for an object that inherits from `hazard_pointer_obj_base`?
options: Immediately calls `delete obj`
Schedules obj for deletion, deferring it until no hazard pointer references it
Marks the object as stale but never deletes it
Decrements a reference count
exercises.explanation: `retire()` hands the object to the hazard pointer domain for deferred deletion. The domain periodically scans registered hazard pointers: if the object's address is not found in any slot, `delete` is called. This is the key distinction from an immediate `delete` — it is where reclamation safety is achieved.

Line 11: the pointer is loaded directly via `head.load()` without `hp.protect()`. Between this load and the subsequent dereference `p->value`, another thread may call `old->retire()` and the runtime may delete the node — resulting in use-after-free. Fix: create a `hazard_pointer` and use `hp.protect(head)` in a do-while loop with a re-check.

exercises.prompt: Why are hazard pointers preferable to `atomic<shared_ptr<T>>` for lock-free structures under heavy read load?
options: shared_ptr does not work in a multithreaded environment
Hazard pointers do not require an atomic refcount update on every read, reducing contention
Hazard pointers automatically prevent ABA without a re-check loop
Hazard pointers use less memory in absolute terms
exercises.explanation: `atomic<shared_ptr<T>>` atomically increments the reference count on every `load` — under heavy read load this operation becomes a bottleneck as threads contend for the cache line holding the count. A hazard pointer performs one atomic store into a slot + load + compare — no shared counter — so it scales significantly better. ABA still requires the re-check loop.

questions.prompt: What does a hazard pointer guarantee for the registered pointer?
options: That the object will never be modified by another thread
That the object will not be freed while the hazard pointer is registered
That the pointer always points to the current value
That no other thread will read the object

questions.prompt: Why does `hp.protect()` return the pointer rather than just registering it?
options: Convenience — so you don't need a separate load call
Because registration and loading must be a single operation (otherwise a race is possible between them)
Because it returns a smart pointer with reference counting
It is purely syntactic sugar

questions.prompt: When is an object for which `retire()` was called actually deleted?
options: Immediately when retire() is called
After the current thread exits
When no hazard pointer in the domain points to that object
After the next call to `std::gc_run()`

questions.prompt: What is the read overhead of hazard pointers compared to a mutex-protected read?
options: Hazard pointers are slower — they perform 2 atomic operations instead of 1
Roughly the same
Hazard pointers are faster under high load: no locking, just one atomic store + load + compare
Hazard pointers are faster because they use no atomic operations at all

lessons.title: std::rcu — Read-Copy-Update
lessons.theory:
Note: `std::rcu` (`<rcu>`, P1122) is part of C++26 but has not yet been implemented by any compiler. All code examples conform to the specification.

## The read-heavy synchronization problem

`std::mutex` serializes all readers and writers. For data structures where reads vastly outnumber writes (routing tables, config objects, system call tables), this is wasteful — readers block each other even though they don't modify data. `std::shared_mutex` helps, but readers still perform atomic operations and may contend.

RCU (Read-Copy-Update) takes a different approach: **reads are completely lock-free and obstruction-free** — no atomic operations, no memory barriers on the read path (in many implementations). Writes are more expensive but rare.

RCU originated in the Linux kernel (used extensively since ~2002) and C++26 standardizes a C++ version via P1122.

## The RCU pattern

**Write-side protocol:**
1. **Read** the current version of the data.
2. **Copy** it to a new allocation.
3. **Update** the copy (the original is untouched — readers still use it safely).
4. **Publish** the new version atomically (`atomic::store(release)`). New readers now see the new version.
5. **Wait** for all pre-publish readers to finish (the "grace period").
6. **Free** the old version — safe now because no reader can reach it anymore.

**Read-side protocol:**
1. Enter a read-side critical section (create an `rcu_reader` guard).
2. Load the pointer (acquire). The object is guaranteed not to be freed during the critical section.
3. Use the object.
4. Exit the critical section (guard destructor).

## C++26 API (spec-based)

**`std::rcu_obj_base<T, Deleter>`** — CRTP base class for RCU-protected objects. Adds a `retire(domain)` method to schedule deletion after the next grace period.

**`std::rcu_domain`** — tracks readers. `std::rcu_default_domain()` returns the global domain.

**`std::rcu_reader`** — RAII guard. Constructor marks the current thread as in a read-side critical section. Destructor exits it. While any `rcu_reader` exists in a thread, no object retired before the reader was constructed will be freed.

**`std::rcu_synchronize(domain)`** — blocks the calling thread until all pre-existing read-side critical sections have ended (waits for one grace period). Called by writers after publishing the new version and before freeing the old one. Non-scalable: use `retire()` instead for high-performance writers.

**`std::rcu_retire(obj, deleter, domain)`** — schedules `obj` for deletion after the next grace period (non-blocking alternative to `rcu_synchronize`). Prefer this over blocking.

**`std::rcu_barrier(domain)`** — waits until all pending `retire()` callbacks have been invoked. Use before program shutdown or domain destruction.

## `rcu_synchronize` vs `retire`

- **`rcu_synchronize(domain)`** — blocking. Simple. Write: publish new → call `rcu_synchronize` → free old. Use for infrequent updates where simplicity beats performance.
- **`retire()`** — non-blocking. Writer returns immediately; runtime defers deletion. Better for high-frequency updates. Use `rcu_barrier` at shutdown.

## RCU vs hazard pointers

| | `std::hazard_pointer` | `std::rcu` |
|---|---|---|
| Reader overhead | Store to HP slot + re-check | RAII guard (often just a counter) |
| Protects | Individual pointers | Entire critical sections |
| Reclamation granularity | Per-object (after all HPs to it clear) | Per domain (after grace period) |
| Memory for old versions | Until all HPs clear | Until grace period ends |
| Best for | Individual pointer protection in lock-free structures | Read-heavy data, config, routing tables |

## When to use

- Read-heavy shared configuration
- Routing tables, DNS caches
- Observer lists, plugin registries
- Any data structure where reads must never block but writes can be slower

examples.title: Reader accessing config via rcu_reader
Writer updating config via retire()

exercises.prompt: What does `std::rcu_reader` guarantee for its entire lifetime?
options: That no other thread can read the data
That no object retired before this reader was constructed will be freed
That the current thread has exclusive access to the data
That write operations are blocked

exercises.prompt: What does `std::rcu_synchronize(domain)` do on the writer side?
options: Immediately deletes all retired objects
Blocks the calling thread until all existing read-side critical sections have ended (the grace period)
Prevents new readers from entering the critical section
Publishes the new version of the data

exercises.prompt: Find the bug in this config-update function:
exercises.explanation: `delete old` is called immediately after publishing the new config, but existing readers may still hold a pointer to `old` inside their `rcu_reader` critical sections. This is undefined behavior (use-after-free). Fix: call `old->retire()` instead of `delete old` — this defers deletion until after the grace period, when all pre-existing readers are guaranteed to have finished.

exercises.prompt: Why is `retire()` preferable to `rcu_synchronize()` for frequent updates?
options: `retire()` frees memory immediately, while rcu_synchronize waits
`retire()` does not block the writer — it returns immediately and allows multiple updates without waiting for a grace period on each one
`retire()` works without an rcu_domain
`rcu_synchronize` is deprecated in C++26

questions.prompt: What is the core idea of the Read-Copy-Update pattern?
options: Readers block writers for the duration of the read
The writer creates a modified copy of the data, atomically publishes it, and frees the old version after the grace period ends
Data is never copied — it is always updated in place
RCU is just a shared_mutex under a different name

questions.prompt: When is it safe to delete the old version of an object after an RCU update?
options: Immediately after atomically publishing the new version
After calling `rcu_barrier()`
After the grace period ends — when all readers that started before the publish have finished
Never — RCU does not delete objects

questions.prompt: Which scenario is `std::rcu` best suited for?
options: Frequent writes with rare reads
Rare writes with frequent concurrent reads (e.g. server configuration)
Message passing between threads
Protecting a single pointer in a lock-free stack

questions.prompt: What is the grace period in the context of RCU?
options: A waiting interval before a write begins
The period during which the runtime waits for all readers that started before the new version was published to finish, before freeing the old version
A timeout between two successive data updates
The lifetime of an rcu_reader object

lessons.title: Memory model & execution model integration
lessons.theory:
## Quick recap of std::memory_order

Five memory orders matter in practice:

| Order | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `memory_order_relaxed` | Atomicity only, no synchronization. Use for per-thread counters. |
| `memory_order_acquire` | This load sees all stores that happened before any `release` in another thread. |
| `memory_order_release` | Stores before this are visible to any `acquire` in another thread. |
| `memory_order_acq_rel` | Both acquire and release — for read-modify-write ops like `fetch_add`. |
| `memory_order_seq_cst` | Total global order. Default. Safest but potentially most expensive. |

The canonical pattern — producer/consumer handoff:

```cpp
// Thread A (producer):
data = 42;                                    // non-atomic
flag.store(true, memory_order_release);       // publish

// Thread B (consumer):
while (!flag.load(memory_order_acquire)) {}   // wait
use(data);                                    // safe: sees data = 42
```

The `release` store in A "synchronizes-with" the `acquire` load in B — everything A wrote before the `release` is visible to B after its `acquire`.

## How hazard pointers use the memory model (connecting m9-l1)

- `hp.protect(atomic_ptr)` does an `acquire` load — the reader sees all data written before the pointer was published.
- When the reclaimer checks hazard pointer slots before freeing, it uses `acquire` semantics — it sees the most recent HP registrations.
- The re-check loop in `hp.protect()` guards against ABA: if the pointer changed between the HP store and the re-read, we retry.

## How RCU uses the memory model (connecting m9-l2)

- The writer publishes the new pointer via `store(release)` — all readers with a matching `load(acquire)` see the complete new object.
- The grace period mechanism ensures that when `rcu_synchronize` returns, all pre-publish readers have completed — this relies on sequencing their exits with a fence.

## Connection to std::execution (Module 6)

Inside a sender pipeline, manual `std::atomic` is not needed — the sender/receiver contract already provides happens-before guarantees:

- `connect(sender, receiver)` + `start(op)` together ensure that the receiver's completion functions see all effects of the sender's work.
- When a scheduler transfers execution to another thread, it implicitly provides acquire/release fencing at the thread boundary.
- **Rule:** use `std::atomic` for shared mutable state *outside* sender pipelines; inside a pipeline, the framework handles synchronization.

## Practical guide — when to use each tool

| Tool | When to use |
|---|---|
| `std::mutex` | Long critical sections, complex invariants, correctness > performance |
| `std::atomic` + acq/rel | Simple flags, one-time init, producer/consumer handoff |
| `std::hazard_pointer` | Lock-free pointer structures needing safe reclamation |
| `std::rcu` | Read-heavy data, config objects, routing tables |
| `std::execution` | Composable async pipelines — don't manage threads manually |

examples.title: Acquire/release pattern (basic)
examples.description: Demonstrates synchronization via a store(release)/load(acquire) pair in a single thread — the memory_order semantics are identical to the multi-threaded case.

examples.title: atomic_thread_fence with relaxed operations (advanced)
examples.description: Explicit memory fences separate atomicity from ordering: relaxed operations provide only atomicity, while the fence provides synchronization.

exercises.prompt: Which memory order combination guarantees that all writes made by the producer thread are visible to the consumer thread after it reads the flag?
options: memory_order_relaxed on both ends
memory_order_release on the flag write and memory_order_acquire on the flag read
memory_order_seq_cst only on the read side
memory_order_acq_rel on the flag in both threads

exercises.prompt: Find the synchronization bug: the consumer may read stale data despite waiting for a non-null pointer.
exercises.explanation: store(memory_order_relaxed) does not synchronize with load(memory_order_relaxed) in the consumer. The consumer may see the new pointer but read stale data (or stale pointee contents). Fix: use memory_order_release on the store and memory_order_acquire on the load to establish the happens-before relationship.

questions.prompt: What does a store(release) + load(acquire) pair on the same atomic object guarantee?
options: That the stored value will not change
That all writes made by the producer thread before the store are visible to the consumer thread after the acquire load
That the operation completes in O(1) cycles
That operations execute in source-code order

questions.prompt: Why is manual std::atomic not needed inside a std::execution sender pipeline?
options: Because senders are single-threaded
Because the sender/receiver contract already provides happens-before guarantees between operations in the pipeline
Because std::atomic is forbidden in coroutines
Because the compiler automatically inserts memory barriers

questions.prompt: When should you prefer std::rcu over std::mutex?
options: When the critical section contains complex invariants
When data changes rarely but is read by many threads simultaneously and blocking readers is unacceptable
When you need strict sequential consistency
When C++26 is unavailable

challenge.title: Implement call_once_atomic
challenge.description: Write a function `call_once_atomic(fn)` that calls `fn` exactly once using `std::atomic<bool>` and `compare_exchange_strong` with appropriate memory orders. The result must be safely visible to all threads that call the function after the first invocation.
